pattern: Since binary representations never have leading zeros, the only monobit values are zero itself and the repunits 2^m - 1, so counting them is a short loop instead of a scan of the whole range.
hints: A positive integer's binary form always starts with a 1 - so for every bit to match, every bit must be 1. Which numbers have an all-ones binary representation?
The all-ones numbers of bit-length m are exactly 2^m - 1 (1, 3, 7, 15, ...); zero is the only number whose representation is all zeros.
Count 1 for zero, then keep doubling-and-subtracting-one (1, 3, 7, 15, ...) while the value stays <= n, counting each one that fits.

edge_inputs.description: n exactly at a repunit boundary
n just below a repunit boundary

stress.description: random n across the whole allowed range
